request.question: Sorry is a 1966 song that was written by Stevie Wright and another Australian musician who moved to Australia with his family as a teenager, but was born where?

title: Chris Cheney
George Young (rock musician)
Sorry (The Easybeats song)
Black Eyed Bruiser (album)
The Wrights (Australian band)
Wedding Ring (song)
She's So Fine
Evie (song)
Harry Bailey
Hard Road (Stevie Wright album)
sentences: Christopher John Cheney (born 2 January 1975) is an Australian rock musician, record producer and studio owner.
He is the founding mainstay guitarist, songwriter and lead vocalist of the psychobilly band, The Living End, which was formed in 1994 with school mate Scott Owen.

Cheney wrote the group's top 20 hits on the ARIA Singles Chart: "Second Solution" / "Prisoner of Society" (1997), "All Torn Down" (1999), "Pictures in the Mirror" (2000), "Roll On" (2001), "One Said to the Other" (2003), "What's on Your Radio" (2005), "Wake Up" (2006) and "White Noise" (2008).
In 2004 Cheney joined the super group The Wrights which put out a cover version of Stevie Wright's epic 11-minute track, "Evie" as a single.
At the APRA Awards of 2009 Cheney won 'Song of the Year' for writing The Living End's track, "White Noise".
In 2005 he married Emma, the couple have two daughters and are co-owners of a recording facility, Red Door Studios.
In 2011 the Cheney family relocated to Los Angeles.
George Redburn Young (born 6 November 1946) is an Australian musician, songwriter and record producer.
Born in Scotland, he moved to Australia with his family as a teenager, and became a naturalised citizen.

He is best known as a member of the 1960s Australian rock band the Easybeats, and as a co-writer of the international hits "Friday on My Mind" and "Love Is in the Air", the latter recorded by John Paul Young (no relation).
Young was also the producer of some work by the Australian hard rock band AC/DC, formed by his younger brothers Malcolm and Angus Young.
"Sorry" is a 1966 song and single by Australian rock group The Easybeats, which was written by band members George Young and Stevie Wright.
It peaked at #1 on the Australian Go-Set's National Top 40 in mid November 1966.
It remained at #1 on the Australian Charts for 2 weeks in November 1966.
Black Eyed Bruiser is the second studio album from Australian singer Stevie Wright.
The album was not as commercially successful as its predecessor "Hard Road" and would be the Wright's final album released with production team Vanda and Young and record label Albert Productions.
The Wrights were a one-off Australian rock music "supergroup".

They consisted of Nic Cester (of Jet), Bernard Fanning (of Powderfinger), Phil Jamieson (of Grinspoon), Kram (of Spiderbait), Chris Cheney (of The Living End), Davey Lane (of You Am I and The Pictures) and Pat Bourke (of Dallas Crane).
They are named after Australian music legend and former Easybeats frontman Stevie Wright, the original performer of the song-trilogy "Evie" which was the group's feature song.
"Wedding Ring" is a song written by Stevie Wright and George Young.
It was originally recorded by the Australian rock group the Easybeats in 1965, whose version reached #6 on the Australian charts.
"She's So Fine" is a song written by Stevie Wright and George Young.
It was originally recorded by the Australian rock group the Easybeats in 1965, whose version reached number three in the Australian charts.
"Evie" is an Australian rock song released as a single in 1974 by Australian singer and former frontman of The Easybeats, Stevie Wright.
It was written by Harry Vanda and George Young.
It has been suggested that it is the first 11-minute song to chart at #1 anywhere in the world.
Harry Richard Bailey (29 October 1922, Picton, New South Wales – 8 September 1985, Mount White, New South Wales) was an Australian psychiatrist and hospital administrator.

He bore the primary responsibility for treatment of mental patients and drug dependent clients via deep sleep therapy and other method at a mental hospital in the Sydney North Shore suburb of Pennant Hills; he was its principal.
The treatment has been linked with the deaths of a total of 85 patients, including 19 who committed suicide.
Bailey committed suicide while he was investigated.

One of Bailey's clients was Australian rock and pop singer and songwriter Stevie Wright, who was being treated for methadone addiction and later committed suicide, a report that was later part of a 2013 documentary on "Australian Story".
Hard Road is the debut solo album from Australian singer Stevie Wright.
The album's first single "Evie (Part 1)" was hugely successful and the title track was later covered on Rod Stewart's 1974 album "Smiler".
The album itself reach #2 on the Australian albums charts in 1974 was the 16th highest selling album in Australia that year.
The compact disc is currently out-of-print and has become quite rare.
A digital edition was available on iTunes as of June, 2014.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Produce a single, concise, structured first-hop evidence report as the `summary`. The summary must do all of the following and only include content that helps determine what to fetch next:

- Enumerate concrete facts found in the provided passages. For each fact include the passage source label and either a very short quoted fragment (up to about 15 words) when a decisive short fact appears or a brief paraphrase otherwise. Keep each fact to one short line.

- Explicitly identify the bridge entity(ies) and any missing or ambiguous fact(s) that prevent answering the question from the given passages. If a name or identifier in the question corresponds to multiple candidate pages present in `passages`, list every candidate variant and mark the name as ambiguous; do not choose between them.

- Prescribe concrete, prioritized next-hop retrieval targets to resolve each missing or ambiguous element. For each target, specify which page(s) or disambiguation page to fetch and what exact section, keyword, or datum to extract (for example: specific field such as birth year, office held, list membership, dates, or the section heading to check). Prioritize targets (1, 2, 3...) when helpful.

- Only produce a final answer in `summary` if every required fact for every entity is present and unambiguous in the supplied passages; otherwise do not produce a final answer and focus on retrieval guidance.

Formatting and content constraints:
- Keep the summary concise (a short paragraph or a few short bullet-like lines).
- Produce only the single output field `summary` and nothing else.
- Preserve source-linked evidence; do not assert facts without a passage label.
- Do not invent facts or assume which ambiguous candidate is intended.
- Avoid copying long verbatim spans; use short quoted fragments when decisive and paraphrase otherwise.
- When an expected authoritative page is missing from `passages`, note that absence and include that page title as a next-hop retrieval target.

Input: fields `question` and `passages`. Output: one field `summary` meeting the requirements above.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

You are to produce exactly one compact second-hop retrieval query that targets the missing bridge or supporting evidence referenced by summary_1. Follow these operational rules when constructing the single `query` string:

- Use visible anchor tokens from `question` or `summary_1` (named works, titles, artist names, years or other identifiers only if present) and append a short retrieval cue naming the bridge sought (e.g., artist, album, recording, genre, season count, passage, supporting quote).
- Emit one concise line (one string assigned to `query`), roughly ≤ 10–12 words, formatted either as a short interrogative that names the anchors or as a compact keyword string (anchors + bridge keywords).
- If summary_1 explicitly requests a missing attribute or passage, emit the focused retrieval query rather than asking for clarification.
- Do not include answers, conclusions, enumerations, provenance notes, speculative language, or user-facing explanations; do not invent entities or add tokens that are not present in the inputs.
- Include year or other identifier tokens only if they appear in the provided inputs and help disambiguate.
- Keep wording retrieval-oriented and terse. Preserve the fallback that if retrieval using this query later fails, downstream steps may request clarification.

Output contract: produce exactly one field `query` whose value is the single compact retrieval query line described above.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single field only):
1. Merge the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into one compact integrated `summary` that preserves the primary answer name(s) and only includes passage-supported facts needed to justify or disambiguate that answer.
2. If `context` already contains a concise answer (a short sentence or a name), keep that content as the first sentence of `summary` when it is consistent with facts in `passages`. If `context` lacks a concise answer, create a concise first sentence that names the primary answer(s), using the canonical labels from `context` when available and otherwise from `passages`.
3. Expand the summary only when `passages` provide explicit, matching second-hop evidence about one or more named entities referenced in `context`. When expansion is appropriate, append at most one short supporting sentence per named entity. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's full name as shown in the inputs and paraphrase up to a few core attributes that are directly present in the `passages` (examples: nationality, a notable award/status, a current affiliation/team, a birth date, membership/partnership relation, or an explicit founding/formation fact).
4. Keep `summary` concise: typically 1–2 sentences for a single entity; allow up to 3 sentences only when multiple distinct named entities each require one supporting sentence. Prioritize the facts most directly necessary to answer the `question`.
5. Only assert facts that are directly supported by the provided `passages` or `context`. Do not introduce new entities, dates, awards, affiliations, roles, or causal claims that are not present in the inputs.
6. Prefer paraphrase over long verbatim copying. Use short quoted phrases only when the exact short phrase (roughly ≤10 words) appears in the `passages` and quoting preserves clarity.
7. If `context` and `passages` conflict, prefer facts that are explicitly present in the `passages`: update the concise answer to match passage-supported content rather than preserving a contradicted `context`.
8. If the retrieved `passages` do not supply explicit supporting evidence for named entities in `context`, keep the `summary` concise and do not expand or speculate.
9. Output exactly one field named `summary` whose value is the integrated post-retrieval summary text. Do not output additional fields, metadata, or commentary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Follow these operational rules when producing `answer`:
- Use only the content of `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2` as your evidential basis. Do not introduce new facts or external information.
- If both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, set `answer` to exactly one lowercase token: yes or no. The `answer` field must contain nothing else (no punctuation, capitalization, quotes, explanation, or additional text).
- Decide which token to emit by aggregating the visible summaries only. Treat `summary_2` as the later integrated evidence; do not let unresolved language in `summary_1` alone prevent emitting the single-token result if both summaries now unambiguously agree.
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, incomplete, hedged such that the relation is unresolved, or if the question is non-binary, do not apply the single-token rule; instead follow the agent's existing final-answer fallback behavior for unresolved or non-binary cases (which may produce a fuller response or an uncertainty signal).

Produce only the `answer` field as specified.

response.hop1_query: Sorry is a 1966 song that was written by Stevie Wright and another Australian musician who moved to Australia with his family as a teenager, but was born where?
hop1_docs: Sorry (The Easybeats song) | "Sorry" is a 1966 song and single by Australian rock group The Easybeats, which was written by band members George Young and Stevie Wright.  It peaked at #1 on the Australian Go-Set's National Top 40 in mid November 1966.  It remained at #1 on the Australian Charts for 2 weeks in November 1966.

Stevie Wright | Stephen Carlton "Stevie" Wright (20 December 1947 – 27 December 2015), formerly billed as Little Stevie, was an English-born musician and songwriter who has been called Australia's first international pop star.  During 1964–69 he was lead singer of Sydney-based rock and roll band the Easybeats, widely regarded as the greatest Australian pop band of the 1960s.

Kram (musician) | Kram (born Mark Anthony Maher in Finley, New South Wales) is the drummer/singer of Australian band Spiderbait.  His stage name is an anagram of his first name; "Kram" is simply "Mark" spelled backwards.  Kram has appeared at the 2004 benefit concert for the South-East Asian tsunami, WaveAid, as a member of supergroup The Wrights who performed Stevie Wright's 3 part classic, "Evie".

A Place in the Sun (Stevie Wonder song) | "A Place in the Sun" is a 1966 soul single by American and Motown musician Stevie Wonder.  Written by Ronald Miller and Bryan Wells, it was one of Wonder's first songs to contain social commentary.  "A Place in the Sun" was his third Top Ten hit since 1963 and hit #9 on the "Billboard" pop singles chart and # 3 on the R&B charts.  Stevie Wonder also recorded a version of the song in Italian titled "Il Sole è di Tutti" ("The Sun is for Everyone").

George Young (rock musician) | George Redburn Young (born 6 November 1946) is an Australian musician, songwriter and record producer.  Born in Scotland, he moved to Australia with his family as a teenager, and became a naturalised citizen.  He is best known as a member of the 1960s Australian rock band the Easybeats, and as a co-writer of the international hits "Friday on My Mind" and "Love Is in the Air", the latter recorded by John Paul Young (no relation).  Young was also the producer of some work by the Australian hard rock band AC/DC, formed by his younger brothers Malcolm and Angus Young.

Wedding Ring (song) | "Wedding Ring" is a song written by Stevie Wright and George Young.  It was originally recorded by the Australian rock group the Easybeats in 1965, whose version reached #6 on the Australian charts.

Evie (song) | "Evie" is an Australian rock song released as a single in 1974 by Australian singer and former frontman of The Easybeats, Stevie Wright.  It was written by Harry Vanda and George Young.  It has been suggested that it is the first 11-minute song to chart at #1 anywhere in the world.
hop1_titles: A Place in the Sun (Stevie Wonder song)
response.summary_1:
- [1] "written by band members George Young and Stevie Wright" (quote).
- [5] "Born in Scotland, he moved to Australia with his family as a teenager" (quote).
- Bridge entity: George Young (co-writer of "Sorry") — birthplace clearly given.
- Missing/ambiguous facts: none; George Young's birthplace is unambiguous in the passages.
- Final answer: The other co-writer, George Young, was born in Scotland.
hop2_docs: Whitney Young Birthplace and Museum | The Whitney Young Birthplace and Museum was the birthplace and childhood home of Whitney M. Young, Jr., an American civil rights leader.  The simple wooden house in Shelby County, Kentucky, near Louisville, is on the campus of the former Lincoln Institute, an all-black high school that Young attended and Young's father led.
George Westinghouse Jr. Birthplace and Boyhood Home | George Westinghouse Jr.  Birthplace and Boyhood Home

George Washington Birthplace National Monument | The George Washington Birthplace National Monument is a national monument in Westmoreland County, Virginia, United States.  This site was established in the 17th century as a colonial plantation by Englishman John Washington, George Washington's great-grandfather.  George Washington was born here on February 22, 1732.  He lived here until age three, returning later to live here as a teenager.

Distinguished Young Women | Distinguished Young Women, formerly known as America's Junior Miss, is a national non-profit organization that provides scholarship opportunities to high school senior girls.  Depending on the schedule of the various state and local programs, young women are eligible during the summer preceding their senior year in high school.  This program is designed to provide young women with the opportunity and support needed to succeed before, during, and after attending college.  Since its creation in 1958, over 700,000 young ladies have participated in competitions spanning the United States.  Contestants compete in the categories of Interview (25%), Scholastics (25%), Talent (20%), Fitness (15%), and Self-Expression (15%).  Each state hosts a state program in which the chosen representative advances to the national program, held in the program's birthplace of Mobile, Alabama.

Henry George Birthplace | Henry George Birthplace is a historic home located in the Washington Square West neighborhood of Philadelphia, Pennsylvania.  It was built in 1801, and is a three story brick rowhouse in a late Federal style.  The layout is typical of a Philadelphia rowhouse with a "front building" and "back building."  It also has a "piazza."  Noted journalist and philosopher Henry George (1839-1897) was born in the house in 1839, and resided there until 1849.

George Young (Methodist minister) | Reverend George Young (December 31, 1821 – August 1, 1910) was a Canadian Methodist minister and author noted for his role in the Red River Rebellion of 1869–1870.  He was a supporter of the pro-Canadian faction led by John Christian Schultz.  He is remembered today largely for his memoir of the rebellion "Manitoba memories; leaves from my life in the prairie province, 1868–1884".

George Williams (YMCA) | Sir George Williams (11 October 18216 November 1905) was an English philanthropist and founder of the YMCA.  The oldest and largest youth charity in the world, its aim is to support young people to belong, contribute and thrive in their communities.
hop2_titles: George Westinghouse Jr. Birthplace and Boyhood Home
response.summary_2: The other co-writer, George Young, was born in Scotland.
